steps.title: Identify the contrast between the two studies
steps.content:
Henry found that investment increased significantly in the three years following liberalization, while Levine and Zervos found that liberalization did not lead to enduring (lasting) increases over the full 1976–1993 period.

The passage signals this contrast with “however” and asks us to synthesize both findings with “Taken together, these results suggest.”

steps.title: Reconcile both findings to find the completion
steps.content:
If investment grows in the first three years (Henry) but doesn't endure long-term (Levine and Zervos), the combined picture is an initial boost that diminishes over time. Choice D directly states this pattern: a boost that fades over time.

The other three choices fail to capture this reconciliation. Choice A claims consistency, which contradicts the evidence of fading effects; choice B invokes economists' expectations without support in the passage; choice C reverses the timeline, suggesting benefits come later rather than initially.

That matches choice D — D is correct.
